tossups.question: This director’s motif of “immersion” inspired a film’s repeated close-ups on a CRT showing a news report about a Marian apparition on a water tank. The protagonist of a film by this director washes out her bright blonde hair dye to avoid suspicion for a hit-and-run, despite being unsure herself if what she killed was a dog or a pottery worker’s child. The cacophony of pool chairs being dragged on metal tiles dominates an opening scene by this director, in which old people stagger like zombies towards a wine glass. After being told not to worry about directing action scenes, this filmmaker declined Marvel’s offer for Black Widow. In 2017, this director adapted an Antonio (*) di Benedetto novel about a Spanish bureaucrat’s secluded tenure in Paraguay. Two bourgeois families waste away at a decrepit, swampy estate in a film shot in this director’s hometown of Salta. For 10 points, The Headless Woman, Zama, and La Ciénaga (“see-AY-nah-gah”) are films by what Argentine director?
tossups.question_sanitized: This director's motif of "immersion" inspired a film's repeated close-ups on a CRT showing a news report about a Marian apparition on a water tank. The protagonist of a film by this director washes out her bright blonde hair dye to avoid suspicion for a hit-and-run, despite being unsure herself if what she killed was a dog or a pottery worker's child. The cacophony of pool chairs being dragged on metal tiles dominates an opening scene by this director, in which old people stagger like zombies towards a wine glass. After being told not to worry about directing action scenes, this filmmaker declined Marvel's offer for Black Widow. In 2017, this director adapted an Antonio (*) di Benedetto novel about a Spanish bureaucrat's secluded tenure in Paraguay. Two bourgeois families waste away at a decrepit, swampy estate in a film shot in this director's hometown of Salta. For 10 points, The Headless Woman, Zama, and La Cienaga ("see-AY-nah-gah") are films by what Argentine director?

tossups.question: A defensive structure used in one of these campaigns inspired a series of copycat designs whose creators mistakenly translated the original structure’s name as “hammer” instead of “myrtle.” Along with the Letters of Junius, criticism of the British government’s timid response to one of these campaigns prompted the collapse of the Grafton ministry. The losing commander in one of these campaigns spent the next 20 years living off a pension granted by George III and hanging out with political supporters like James (*) Boswell. During one of these campaigns that ended with the surrender of Raphaël de Casabianca at Calvi, Horatio Nelson went partially blind in his right eye. The Comte de Vaux (“VOH”) conducted one of these campaigns in 1769 that forced the resistance leader Pasquale (“pass-KWAH-lay”) Paoli to go into exile. For 10 points, name these military expeditions that sought to capture an island off the southern coast of France.
tossups.answer: invasions of Corsica [accept equivalents in place of “invasions”, e.g. “conquests” or “occupations”] {The lead-in refers to the Torra di Mortella, which was the model for the British Empire’s Martello towers.}
tossups.answer_sanitized: invasions of Corsica [accept equivalents in place of "invasions", e.g. "conquests" or "occupations"] {The lead-in refers to the Torra di Mortella, which was the model for the British Empire's Martello towers.}
tossups.question_sanitized: A defensive structure used in one of these campaigns inspired a series of copycat designs whose creators mistakenly translated the original structure's name as "hammer" instead of "myrtle." Along with the Letters of Junius, criticism of the British government's timid response to one of these campaigns prompted the collapse of the Grafton ministry. The losing commander in one of these campaigns spent the next 20 years living off a pension granted by George III and hanging out with political supporters like James (*) Boswell. During one of these campaigns that ended with the surrender of Raphael de Casabianca at Calvi, Horatio Nelson went partially blind in his right eye. The Comte de Vaux ("VOH") conducted one of these campaigns in 1769 that forced the resistance leader Pasquale ("pass-KWAH-lay") Paoli to go into exile. For 10 points, name these military expeditions that sought to capture an island off the southern coast of France.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer the following about follicularly challenged beings from history:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, answer the following about follicularly challenged beings from history:
parts: A bald Frankish king with this name took power after the death of Louis II. Another man with this name had the last Merovingian (“mair-uh-VIN-jee-in”) king tonsured and gained the nickname “the hammer” after winning the Battle of Tours (“tour”).
A bal<u>d king w</u>ith this n<u>ame hel</u>ped Malcom II de<u>feat the</u> English at the Battle of Carham. Adam of Usk recorded the death of a rebel with this name from natural causes.

</u> Hairless orchid d<u>ogs na</u>med after this empire w<u>ere one o</u>f the six dog breeds recorded by its colonizers. Figure-eight knots made of hair were used to transmit messages across this empire.
parts_sanitized: A bald Frankish king with this name took power after the death of Louis II. Another man with this name had the last Merovingian ("mair-uh-VIN-jee-in") king tonsured and gained the nickname "the hammer" after winning the Battle of Tours ("tour").
A bald king with this name helped Malcom II defeat the English at the Battle of Carham. Adam of Usk recorded the death of a rebel with this name from natural causes.
Hairless orchid dogs named after this empire were one of the six dog breeds recorded by its colonizers. Figure-eight knots made of hair were used to transmit messages across this empire.
answers: Owain [accept Owain Foel; accept Owain the Bald; accept Owain Glyndwr; accept Owen the Bald; accept Owen Glendower; prompt on Eugenius Calvus]
Incan Empire [accept Peruvian Incan hairless dog; accept Peruvian Inca Orchid]
answers_sanitized: Charles [accept Charles the Bald; accept Charles Martel]
Owain [accept Owain Foel; accept Owain the Bald; accept Owain Glyndwr; accept Owen the Bald; accept Owen Glendower; prompt on Eugenius Calvus]
Incan Empire [accept Peruvian Incan hairless dog; accept Peruvian Inca Orchid]

bonuses.leadin: The night before the Battle of Droizy (“dr’wah-ZEE”), this woman ordered a row of foot soldiers to march at the front of her army carrying tree branches to trick her rival’s sentries into thinking they were part of the forest. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this woman whom Gregory of Tours (“TOOR”) characterized as sadistic and cruel in part due to her actions during her long-running feud with Brunhilda. This woman acted as regent for her son Chlothar II (“the second”) after her husband’s death.

Fredegund and Brunhilda were the queens of Neustria (“NEW-stree-uh”) and Austrasia (“awss-TRAY-zhuh”), two states of these people that were carved out of the realm of Clovis I (“the first”). Ruling dynasties of these people’s kingdoms included the Merovingians (“mare-uh-VIN-jee-unz”) and Carolingians (“care-uh-LIN-jee-unz”).

This mayor of the palace became the de facto ruler of a reunited Frankish kingdom after winning the Battle of Tertry. This man disinherited the sons of his second wife Alpaida at the behest of his first wife Plectrude, but one of those sons seized power anyway by defeating this man’s grandson Theudoald in a civil war.
answers: Franks [or Frankish people; prompt on Germanic peoples; reject “French people” or “German people”]
Pepin II (“the second”) [or Pepin of Herstal; or Pepin the Middle; prompt on Pepin] {Pepin II and Alpaida were the parents of Charles Martel.}
answers_sanitized: Fredegunda [or Fredegundis]
Franks [or Frankish people; prompt on Germanic peoples; reject "French people" or "German people"]
Pepin II ("the second") [or Pepin of Herstal; or Pepin the Middle; prompt on Pepin] {Pepin II and Alpaida were the parents of Charles Martel.}
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The night before the Battle of Droizy ("dr'wah-ZEE"), this woman ordered a row of foot soldiers to march at the front of her army carrying tree branches to trick her rival's sentries into thinking they were part of the forest. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this woman whom Gregory of Tours ("TOOR") characterized as sadistic and cruel in part due to her actions during her long-running feud with Brunhilda. This woman acted as regent for her son Chlothar II ("the second") after her husband's death.

Fredegund and Brunhilda were the queens of Neustria ("NEW-stree-uh") and Austrasia ("awss-TRAY-zhuh"), two states of these people that were carved out of the realm of Clovis I ("the first"). Ruling dynasties of these people's kingdoms included the Merovingians ("mare-uh-VIN-jee-unz") and Carolingians ("care-uh-LIN-jee-unz").

This mayor of the palace became the de facto ruler of a reunited Frankish kingdom after winning the Battle of Tertry. This man disinherited the sons of his second wife Alpaida at the behest of his first wife Plectrude, but one of those sons seized power anyway by defeating this man's grandson Theudoald in a civil war.

bonuses.leadin: In a novel titled for this country, a woman is brought here from Paris to "transform [it] into a country exactly like France." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country home to the writers Delfina Acosta and Lourdes Espinola. This country's president has an affair with Eliza Lynch in a historical novel by Lily Tuck.
Antonio di Benedetto's novel about 18th-century Paraguay, Zama, was adapted into a tragicomic 2017 film by this Argentine director of The Headless Woman and La ciénaga.

A man with this profession molests the teen Amalia in Lucrecia Martel's film Holy Girl. In Ha Jin's Waiting, a man with this job waits 18 years so he can get a divorce and marry his colleague, the nurse Manna.
answers: Paraguay [or República del Paraguay; or Tetã Paraguái; accept The News from Paraguay; accept Gloria del Paraguay]
answers_sanitized: Paraguay [or Republica del Paraguay; or Teta Paraguai; accept The News from Paraguay; accept Gloria del Paraguay]
Lucrecia Martel
doctors [or physicians; accept equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: According to legend, this town’s name comes from the name taken by the Andalusian general Mirat, after he converted to Christianity when an eagle dropped a fat trout from the sky, causing him to surrender to the “Queen of the Sky.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this sleepy Occitan town home to the Chateau Fort, which was surrendered to Britain in the 1360 CE Treaty of Bretigny.

Saracens were still hanging around Lourdes at places like Mirambel castle, years after this Mayor of the Palace had driven them out at the 732 CE Battle of Tours. His son Pepin the Short was the father of Charlemagne.

Lourdes became a major pilgrimage site in 1858 after this local miller’s daughter experienced Marian apparitions at the grotto of Massabielle. Church authorities have claimed that her remains are internally incorrupt.
answers_sanitized: Lourdes [or Lorda]
Charles Martel [prompt on Charles]
Saint Bernadette Soubirous [accept either underlined part]

bonuses.leadin: This film’s star walked out of an interview when asked if it might “perversely end up inspiring exactly the kind of people it’s about.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Todd Phillips film that won the Golden Lion at the 2019 Venice Film Festival. In this film, Joaquin Phoenix plays a failed stand-up comedian named Arthur Fleck, who then becomes the title DC Comics villain.
The chair of the 2019 Venice jury was this director of La Ciénaga and The Headless Woman. Her most recent film, Zama, was the Argentinian submission for Best Foreign Language Film in 2017.

Joker was partially inspired by this director’s film The King of Comedy. This man’s most recent film eschewed the use of motion tracking markers for its extensive CGI, which was used to “de-age” its main actors.
answers: Martin (Charles) Scorsese (The second film is The Irishman.)
answers_sanitized: Joker
Lucrecia Martel
Martin (Charles) Scorsese (The second film is The Irishman.)

bonuses.leadin: This ruler legendarily ordered his first wife, Elisabeth of Vendome, to be burnt at the stake over accusations of adultery. For 10 points each:
answers: Fulk Nerra [or Fulk III of Anjou or Foulque Nerra or Fulk the Black; prompt on Fulk of Anjou or Nerra alone]
parts: Name this early count of Anjou who pioneered a strategy of coupling territorial expansion with the creation of an extensive network of defensive fortifications, monasteries, and abbeys.

Fulk Nerra is celebrated as one of the most prominent builders of these buildings in all of medieval Europe, ordering the construction of over 100 of these fortified residences throughout the Loire valley during his reign.

Fulk Nerra’s son and successor, Geoffrey II of Anjou, received this epithet for his aggressively expansionist military policies. An earlier French leader earned this epithet for his defeat of Abd-ar-Rahman.
answers_sanitized: Fulk Nerra [or Fulk III of Anjou or Foulque Nerra or Fulk the Black; prompt on Fulk of Anjou or Nerra alone]
castle (s) [prompt on forts]
Martel [accept "the hammer"; accept Geoffroy Martel or Charles Martel]

bonuses.leadin: Eudes of Aquitaine swore fealty to the victor of this battle. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 732 battle in which repeated cavalry charges against the winning side’s entirely infantry army led to the death of Abd ar Rahman, whose army then retreated south.

This son of Pepin of Herstal was nicknamed “the Hammer” and led the French to victory at Tours despite being outnumbered. He was technically only the Mayor of the Palace, but his son Pepin the Short became king.
This eldest son of Lothair of France nicknamed “The Lazy” was the last Carolingian king of West Francia. After dying childless, Hugh Capet was elected to succeed him.
answers: Charles Martel [or Carolus Martellus; or Karl Martel; prompt on Charles, Carolus, or Karl]
answers_sanitized: Battle of Tours [or the Battle of Poitiers]
Charles Martel [or Carolus Martellus; or Karl Martel; prompt on Charles, Carolus, or Karl]
Louis V of France [prompt on Louis]

This son of Pepin of Herstal was nicknamed "the Hammer" and led the French to victory at Tours despite being outnumbered. He was technically only the Mayor of the Palace, but his son Pepin the Short became king.
This eldest son of Lothair of France nicknamed "The Lazy" was the last Carolingian king of West Francia. After dying childless, Hugh Capet was elected to succeed him.

bonuses.leadin: Edward Gibbon called this man "the hero of the age". For 10 points each:
parts: What man received a cognomen meaning "the hammer" after winning the Battle of Tours?
Charles Martel's son was this Mayor of the Palace, who deposed Childeric III and declared himself King of the Franks. He was succeeded by his son Charlemagne.

After the Umayyads were defeated by the Franks, they would, under this ruler, eventually establish an emirate in Cordoba that would survive until 1031 and be distinct from the main Caliphate of Damascus. The first ruler of the later Caliphate of Cordoba also had this name.
answers: Abd al-Rahman I [or Abd ar-Rahman I; or Abdul Rahman I; or Abdar Rahman I; or Abderraman I]
answers_sanitized: Charles Martel [prompt on partial answers]
Pepin the Short [or Pepin the Younger; prompt on Pepin]
Abd al-Rahman I [or Abd ar-Rahman I; or Abdul Rahman I; or Abdar Rahman I; or Abderraman I]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Edward Gibbon called this man "the hero of the age". For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: What man received a cognomen meaning "the hammer" after winning the Battle of Tours?
Charles Martel's son was this Mayor of the Palace, who deposed Childeric III and declared himself King of the Franks. He was succeeded by his son Charlemagne.

After the Umayyads were defeated by the Franks, they would, under this ruler, eventually establish an emirate in Cordoba that would survive until 1031 and be distinct from the main Caliphate of Damascus. The first ruler of the later Caliphate of Cordoba also had this name.

bonuses.leadin: At the Battle of Ambleve, this man decisively defeated his Neustrian and Frisian rivals. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Majordormo whose other military victories include one against Odo of Aquitaine at Soissons, and another against the forces of Abd al-Rahman in 732.

Charles Martel was a member of this Frankish dynasty, which was also ruled by Charlemagne. This family had previously served as Mayors of the Palace under the do-nothing kings of the Merovingian Dynasty.

Much of Charlemagne’s administration was carried out by these officials, whose name meant “envoys of the lord.” They carried out administrative tasks in parts of the empire the King could not reach himself.
answers_sanitized: Charles Martel [or Charles the Hammer]
Carolingian Dynasty
Missi Dominici [accept Missus Dominicus]
